text.title: structured-output-mode-picker Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for picking the right constrained-decoding mode for a given agent stage.

rule.statement: Pick the mode by the output consumer, not by which API the team learned first: deterministic extraction → SO strict; agent action / dispatch → tool call; custom DSL or local-model output → grammar; legacy fallback → json-mode (only when strict SO is unsupported on the chosen provider).

rationale.source: BuildMVPFast 2026 production guide; Vellum mode comparison; XGrammar paper.

rule.rationale: Production studies consistently show silent correctness regressions when json-mode is used for typed extraction (passes `json.loads`, fails Pydantic). XGrammar shows 100x speedups vs alternatives on local DSL workloads — the wrong mode is not just incorrect, it is slow.
rule.reference: Four side-by-side calls — one per mode — to compare locally.

rule.statement: Never use `response_format={"type": "json_object"}` for typed extraction tasks where downstream code parses the result into a Pydantic / dataclass model. The format guarantees only valid JSON, not schema compliance.
rule.rationale: json-mode produces valid-but-wrong outputs that pass `json.loads()` and fail downstream typing. The team only discovers the regression in prod data with bad shapes.

rule.statement: Never use SO strict for an action-dispatch task where the LLM must choose which function to call. Use tool-call mode — the function name comes back in the response and feeds the dispatcher directly.
rule.rationale: SO strict forces the model to fit one schema; tool-call lets the model pick from N schemas plus a function name. Using SO for dispatch wastes the routing affordance the ecosystem already provides.

rule.statement: For non-JSON outputs (SQL, regex, custom DSLs, domain grammars) use grammar mode (XGrammar / Outlines / GBNF). Do not detour through JSON or tool-call.
rule.rationale: Detouring through JSON adds two failure modes (JSON parse + JSON-to-DSL convert) and wastes tokens encoding the DSL inside JSON strings. Grammar mode constrains the generation directly to the target CFG.

rule.statement: Use json-mode only when the chosen provider does not support strict SO AND the output consumer is downstream code that can tolerate per-batch failures. Document the fallback in the decision-record with a tracking issue to migrate when strict SO becomes available.
rule.rationale: json-mode without a schema is now the worst of the four for typed extraction; it survives only as a fallback for providers behind the curve.
